pick.rationale: UConn enters as a dominant 2-seed with an elite SRS of 22.92 ranking 12th nationally and the 24th-ranked defense, having navigated the 41st-toughest schedule while compiling a 29-5 record. Furman's SRS of -3.24 ranks 198th with a KenPom rating of 191 against the 289th-ranked schedule, revealing a team significantly overmatched at this level. While the Paladins captured the SoCon tournament championship as a 6-seed behind Cooper Bowser's heroics, the 186-position gap in SRS and massive strength-of-schedule disparity make a 15-over-2 upset virtually impossible against a UConn squad with championship pedigree under Dan Hurley.

reasoningStep.title: UConn vs Furman Comprehensive Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Evaluated advanced metrics showing UConn as an elite 2-seed (12th SRS, 24th defense) against Furman's below-average profile (198th SRS, 191 KenPom). The 186-position SRS gap and massive schedule strength disparity (41st vs 289th SOS) with Furman's negative efficiency margin reveal a prohibitive mismatch. Minor UConn injury concerns (Jaylin Stewart uncertain) do not meaningfully alter the calculus given the structural advantages.

Furman: 22-12 record, SRS -3.24 (198th), KenPom rank 191, SOS -6.14 (289th), Adj EM -1.99
Injury report: Jaylin Stewart (UConn) uncertain for first round, Silas Demary Jr. in good shape
Historical context: 2 seeds win approximately 92% vs 15 seeds

The Huskies are the No. 2 seed and will face the No. 15 Furman Paladins on Friday in Philadelphia. Why it matters UConn is looking to reclaim the national title after missing out on a three-peat in 2025. The health of Stewart and Demary could be crucial to the Huskies' chances of making a deep tournament run. Demary in particular is a key contributor, leading the team in assists and steals. The details Forward Jaylin Stewart has not played since a Feb. 21 game against Villanova and missed the entire Big East tournament. Coach Hurley said it 'will be close' on whether Stewart can play in the first round. Meanwhile, junior guard Silas Demary Jr. is reported to be 'in pretty good shape'. Demary is second on the team in usage and leads the Huskies in assists and steals. Stewart has not played since a Feb. 21 game against Villanova. UConn will face Furman in the first round on Friday, March 21, 2026. The players Dan Hurley The head coach of the UConn Huskies men's basketball team. toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://www.greenvilleonline.com/story/sports/college/furman/2026/03/10/march-madness-furman-basketball-southern-conference-tournament-cooper-bowser/89067306007/ and extracted 3000 characters of text.

result.text: How Cooper Bowser led Furman basketball back to March Madness Skip to main content Home Sports High Schools Clemson USC Furman Paladins Greenville Drive Swamp Rabbits Fantasy Sports SAIL Swimming USA TODAY Sports How Cooper Bowser led Furman basketball back to March Madness Zachary Huber Hendersonville Times-News Show Caption Hide Caption Furman Paladins ribbon cutting and open house a home sweet home event at Timmons Arena Furman basketball teams played last season on the road, and now enjoy their $40 million renovated Timmons Arena Furman defeated East Tennessee State to win the 2026 Southern Conference Tournament championship. Cooper Bowser led the Paladins with a game-high 21 points and 11 rebounds. The victory secures Furman's first appearance in the NCAA Tournament since 2023. ASHEVILLE, NC — Tears fell from Cooper Bowser's eyes as Furman basketball made the trip from the hotel to the arena for its Southern Conference quarterfinal matchup against Samford on March 7. He teared up because the last memory he had of playing in Asheville was a bad one — the 2025 SoCon championship game, where Furman lost to Wofford. "That was the motivation all offseason for me," Bowser said. "A lot of people went home. I stayed here for the whole month of May, just trying to get stronger and better." His hard work paid off as the No. 6-seeded Paladins reached the 2026 SoCon Tournament title game, where they beat top-seeded East Tennessee State 76-61 at Harrah's Cherokee Center on March 9. With the victory — in which he had a game-high 21 points and 11 rebounds, his first double-double of the season — Furman (22-12) guaranteed its spot in March Madness for the first time since 2023. The 6-foot-11 forward started the game strong against the Buccaneers (23-11), scoring 14 first-half points on 6-for-6 shooting from the field. He kept his hot shooting going in the second half, knocking down two more shots and didn't miss until there were 10 minutes left in the game. Furman coach Bob Richey said Bowser had volunteered to come out of the starting lineup ahead of the quarterfinal, believing the Paladins needed to play smaller with four guards instead of three. The junior wanted to come out instead of one of the senior starters. "That's how life works," Richey said. "When you give, you get. He gave away his starting opportunity in that Samford game, then started the last two games due to matchups. He was rewarded tonight with that performance." Bowser said he isn't an emotional person. "I don't cry that much," he said. "A wave of emotions hit me. I started tearing up and started crying, trying to make sure no one saw me." Bowser was all smiles walking off the court, through a 20-minute news conference and to the bus after the championship game. He now has a positive memory of playing at Harrah's Cherokee Center — one where he led the team to its eighth SoCon Tournament championship and a place in the 2026 NCAA Tournament. Zachary Huber is a high school sports reporter f

result.text: How Cooper Bowser led Furman basketball back to March Madness Skip to main content Home Sports High Schools Clemson USC Furman Paladins Greenville Drive Swamp Rabbits Fantasy Sports SAIL Swimming USA TODAY Sports How Cooper Bowser led Furman basketball back to March Madness Zachary Huber Hendersonville Times-News Show Caption Hide Caption Furman Paladins ribbon cutting and open house a home sweet home event at Timmons Arena Furman basketball teams played last season on the road, and now enjoy their $40 million renovated Timmons Arena Furman defeated East Tennessee State to win the 2026 Southern Conference Tournament championship. Cooper Bowser led the Paladins with a game-high 21 points and 11 rebounds. The victory secures Furman's first appearance in the NCAA Tournament since 2023. ASHEVILLE, NC — Tears fell from Cooper Bowser's eyes as Furman basketball made the trip from the hotel to the arena for its Southern Conference quarterfinal matchup against Samford on March 7. He teared up because the last memory he had of playing in Asheville was a bad one — the 2025 SoCon championship game, where Furman lost to Wofford. "That was the motivation all offseason for me," Bowser said. "A lot of people went home. I stayed here for the whole month of May, just trying to get stronger and better." His hard work paid off as the No. 6-seeded Paladins reached the 2026 SoCon Tournament title game, where they beat top-seeded East Tennessee State 76-61 at Harrah's Cherokee Center on March 9. With the victory — in which he had a game-high 21 points and 11 rebounds, his first double-double of the season — Furman (22-12) guaranteed its spot in March Madness for the first time since 2023. The 6-foot-11 forward started the game strong against the Buccaneers (23-11), scoring 14 first-half points on 6-for-6 shooting from the field. He kept his hot shooting going in the second half, knocking down two more shots and didn't miss until there were 10 minutes left in the game. Furman coach Bob Richey said Bowser had volunteered to come out of the starting lineup ahead of the quarterfinal, believing the Paladins needed to play smaller with four guards instead of three. The junior wanted to come out instead of one of the senior starters. "That's how life works," Richey said. "When you give, you get. He gave away his starting opportunity in that Samford game, then started the last two games due to matchups. He was rewarded tonight with that performance." Bowser said he isn't an emotional person. "I don't cry that much," he said. "A wave of emotions hit me. I started tearing up and started crying, trying to make sure no one saw me." Bowser was all smiles walking off the court, through a 20-minute news conference and to the bus after the championship game. He now has a positive memory of playing at Harrah's Cherokee Center — one where he led the team to its eighth SoCon Tournament championship and a place in the 2026 NCAA Tournament. Zachary Huber is a high school sports reporter f
